transcript.transcript: Alright, so we were talking about your father working in Rocky Hill. Connecticut Foundry. Connecticut Foundry. And then that closing down and then him working on Homestead. Philbrook, Booth & Spencer on Homestead Avenue, which was another foundry which is vacant now. Right. Is it the building's vacant? The whole building I think the Salvation Army is a part of the building that they manage the salvage. And the reason is that with the changing times and the EPA laws changed. What exactly did they do in the foundry? They made, my father worked on cupola. What is that? A huge pot where they would put wood and stone or whatever metal. The pot will go up into a huge furnace inside. The cupola was outside. You go inside and it was like a metal factory. Okay. Or an iron ore factory. They made tops for sewer. Okay. The round sewer tops. Oh, I should stop. Oh, no. Go ahead. Are you showing me something? Oh, okay. Alright, so Mr. Brown has gone upstairs to show me something I think related to his father. Earlier, he reminded me about the Les Payne book: The Dead Are Arising. And he's featured in there on page 290 and has reminded me to talk about Malcolm X coming to Bellevue Square and coming to Hartford. Oh that's fine. What is this? Is this something that was hewn by your father? Yeah. As a gift to you? So somebody-- Connecticut Foundry Company, 1988. So I want you to hold it up in front of the camera, and I want you to describe it for me. Now, this is a book end. Is it part of a pair? There are two of them. Yeah. And about 5 or 6 years ago, a young lady there in Rocky Hill in that a location, when she heard that my dad had worked down there she had found this in an antique shop, and she presented it to me. This is a lot of stuff that my dad did and other men. You said, this is the kind of things that they would do? Yeah. This is iron ore, right? Yes. That's very heavy. And you felt it? Yeah. Heavy. But, I was very surprised that one was given to me. It was given to a young man by a young lady who knew the young man. It was bookends. Yeah. I said something earlier about my family. What is the title of that book? Carnival of Blood: Dueling, Lynchings, and Murder in South Carolina, 1880 to 1920. Who is the author? John Hammond Moore. Do you want to read it? Read the section? Yes, let's read it into the record. The title of the book is, as I said, Carnival of Blood: Dueling, Lynching, and Murder in South Carolina, 1880 to 1920. John Hammond Moore. What actually happened in Denmark, South Carolina, a few months earlier is still a bit hazy, since most accounts fail to agree on who was involved. What is known is that three men died and one later found guilty of murder. It had all began several months before George Stevens, a Black telephone company employee, stole some watermelons from a patch near where he was working. Agent Brown, the owner of the watermelons, chased Stevens, who was shielded by his associates. The case came on trial late on the afternoon of 18th of July, 1919, and when court adjourned for the day, the matter still was unresolved. However, Brown and his brother-in-law, Ozell Anderson, clearly were not happy with the drift of the testimony, and once outside, they began shooting. In the crossfire. Brown, Stevens and an innocent bystander, Henry Murray Ray, the 22-year-old son of Sheriff S.G. Ray, was killed. Two more whites were seriously injured. Carol Mobley, a key witness in the case, being heard, and J. Ralph Thompson, who suffered a shattered arm. Excuse my reading. This is my family's history in Denmark, South Carolina. It's in a book. Yeah. My grandfather. Do the details in the book fit with the family history. You're very wise. What it was is that our family didn't talk about it. The reason I found out why they were traumatized by it. My grandmother, who to me was quiet thunder, what happened was that they were told all the men in my grandfather's family were told that if any of you guys come and get his body, Mr. Brown's body, out of the street, we're going to kill y'all. After he was lynched because they wanted it on display? Yeah. So what happened was that my grandmother, this is the family now saying that my grandmother got a wheelbarrow and went and got my grandfather's body out of the street. Despite the warning. And when she did that, the ignoramuses sick degenerates as they were they told his siblings, "y'all better catch the fastest thing moving out of here or we're going to kill all of you N-words. Now, the account I just read is in a book and it's in a newspaper. I found out this guy, Moore based a lot of his writing on what was in the newspapers at that time. So I gave it some validity that way. But within the family we became persona non grata. My grandfather did because by raising cain for not accepting the fact that a Black man stole a watermelon out of his watermelon patch that he worked hard for, and the Black man was defended by his white counterparts. When they threatened to kill all of my grandfather's brothers and sisters and told them to take the fastest train moving out of there, out of Denmark, South Carolina, they did. It was a huge family and they never forgave my grandfather. Because they were quiet, never forgave him. Never forgave my grandfather for causing them to have to leave their land. So what happens is I'm with my cousins to my father, I didn't write the year down, I blame myself, but, that's when we made connections and found out the situation in terms of our family tree. And I had this book with me. That's a newspaper account I had of the incident. I had this book with me, and they were all surprised to see this book about our family. Because, again, there were ten siblings. George Stevens, charged with stealing some watermelons from Brown, Carol Mobley was a witness for Stevens. But my father was not ever bitter, per se, but he didn't take any nonsense from anybody. But, later on, my late brother became a cop in Hartford. Your late brother? Richard. There were seven boys and two girls. Richard became a cop. My father, in our family, we knew he put him out. He disliked cops. No matter, he disliked anybody in uniform. But he was proud of his son becoming a cop. And that was kind of the demeanor my dad had for me. I mean like but he would never say no to me. Like for instance, I'll never forget, I love my dad as much as I love my mom. We were solid. So what happened was he would go to work at 3:00 in the morning. In the summertime again that's before I became sick, I said "Dad, I want to go to work with you." He said, I'll never forget him saying, boy, "you want to go to work with me?" He said, "you know, I go to work at 3:00 in the morning." I said, "I'm going to go to work with you." The next morning, before he got out of bed, I was out of bed and dressed. I went to work and my mother said, "you know that boy. You should've never told him. If he either say yes or no, but don't come to me now." But he said, "what am I going to do?" He said, "you promised him you're going to take him with you. Take his ass to work." Well, what happened was the experience of going through that foundry. I see my dad worked outside and then realizing that he worked outside of that cupola year round. In the winter? In the winter. I never saw my father, I found out later he would have a nipper of gin. I never saw him drunk. I never saw him put his hands on my mother. He was a tough cat, but respecting others. My father's a man like, for instance, his mother, single mother, right? With all that tragedy. So we're young folks and so we go to we go to Trenton to visit my grandma who lived on Bellevue Avenue. So what happens is that my grandma said, "listen, Bub," his nickname was Bub, "you know, I still go to bed early, but I'll stay up for you until 11:00. But after 11:00, you're on your own." So we go to a pool hall where my dad used to rack the balls at a pool hall, and he was like the gladiator come home. The men in there "Buster!" So I'll never forget it. What did they say? Like "Buster, Buster!" That was his nickname. Okay. My dad's nickname was Buster. "Buster, you really come home?" So now he's having fun and we're-- Where are you? We're blessing in his glory. Because we were in that pool hall. You go to pool hall with him too? Oh, yeah. Me and my brothers, he took us. He used to rack balls there as a kid. He wanted to show us. Yeah. So about a certain time my dad said "I'm leaving." And he said, "dad, you are with your boys, man. You know." He said, "your grandmother said she's going to go to bed. She's going to lock the door at a certain time." He said to us, "now, your grandmother said, she's going to lock that door. She said, I don't know where you cats going to sleep at, but I know where the hell I'm sleeping at tonight. On Bellevue Avenue." My grandmother's like your complexion, mild mannered, quiet thunder. She had a wit about her. Like I said, she smoked a pipe and she had a wit about her. Where she could say some things like my aunt, my late aunt, I can verify it's not just me telling tales. My late aunt Trudy, Trudy Merrill, gave her money for herself. Trudy found out that my grandmother gave money to our Uncle Ted. Oh. Who was a loose cat, right? So, Trudy goes to my grandmother said, "mom, is it true you gave that rascal Ted some money?" Grandma said "Yes. He asked for it." Aunt Trudy said to her, this is I verified it. "I gave that money to you, not for you to give to Ted." My grandmother's response was "here is," and my grandmother kept money, but don't know where she got it, said, "here, you want your money back here." My aunt Trudy said, "mama, you don't understand." "No, I do understand. But didn't you give that money to me to do what I wanted to do with it? You gave me that money to do with what I want to do. If you didn't," well you know the end of the story. That was my grandma. That was our grandmother. How how long did she live? She lived in her, I don't know exactly, in her 70s. We never knew the ages. Yeah. But she was up in her 70s. And she stayed at Bellevue until she died? She stayed on Bellevue Avenue and, yeah, she stayed until she passed. In that same house, I think it was 100 Bellevue Avenue. I'm not sure. And then your father stayed at Homestead Foundry. Yeah. Well. and what happened was they. You said the EPA? The feds moved in and closed the place down, just like they closed down Connecticut Foundry. Oh. Because of the fact that it was, the environmental situation changed. I guess that that lead was producing asbestos or something that was detrimental. Wouldn't they have given them some kind of warning or report. Well, they were not making things that were popular after a while. So they didn't have the political clout they had at one time. The owners of the factory. Plus the EPA laws had changed. But you know there must be some truth to it because many of them buildings on Homestead Avenue nearby Homestead and Albany are still there, I think that the Salvation Army is in some way in the area, but many buildings like that, even at Connecticut Foundry, developers. Won't touch it. Well, it's reprehensible. You can't make any money off the eradication. You'd have to do. Yeah, extensive it's big money. My house here, which was built in '45. This has asbestos shelling on the outside. It was the style. In 1943 it was the thing to do. Well, it would cost me big bucks to have it removed. Yeah. As long as you don't touch it where it creates powder and stuff like that. So I understand why, but have you ever been down by in Rocky Hill to Connecticut Foundry? That whole area down there. It's fantastic. I mean you wonder why they haven't built down there. So that foundry is just? Gone. But the building is? No the land is still there. Right. The building is gone in Rocky Hill, though? The whole area is gone. What happens is because of the, and I and I feel strongly and factually, it's because of the remedial program would have to be if you're a developer, you ain't make no money. But you must understand Hartford being Hartford and being a Hartford native, developers in Hartford that I know of in my generation, they used to bring 85 to 95% of their own money to develop in Hartford. What time period was that? That was, obviously, in my generation, 40s, whatever. Because Hartford was one of the richest cities in the country. But what happens is today that number has flipped. Where developers want you, the taxpayer, to pay me and I'm only bringing 15% of the money. The rest is your money. The sort of public trough. So when you were living in Bellevue Square, and as you got to understand when you were older, was there any sense that this was a temporary housing? No. In Bellevue Square. Yeah. No. And later confirmed by me when prefab housing. This is permanent housing, there's been some alterations. The federal public housing in this country what was that Cabrini-Green housing there in Chicago? They built housing for Black folks. The best analogy I got was from Jesse Jackson, "they put a quarter sized peep hole on a dime sized land." The high rises. But what happened was that, if I remember correctly, they put laboratory rats into that same kind of situation. And all of the ills of Cabrini-Green in Chicago came out. Homicide, infanticide, all of the icides, the negative things came out when they put in laboratory rats. So then, of course, we come to the idea no more high rise. And when developers want to target here in Hartford, they target high rises that are in on prime property. Right? I verified it using your thinking, your research. I spent 11 years as a commissioner for Hartford Housing Authority. I learned so, and I attended things. What did you learn? I learned. We always got the shit end of the stick. Even to the point where we have people who look like us, who bullshitted us, the masses. And I say us, and I don't want to sound like some, but there's a tie that binds us. There's a tie that binds us. And Hartford is key to the elements of this country in terms of rich folks, in terms of generational wealth. It's a crime. We had the Black governors, right? At that time that was established by Mike Peters. We went for that. Even me, until I found out hold on a second. What is it? Every year we have a Thomas Hooker Day started by Mike Peters. Why? Probably some folks related to Hooker convinced him that there was money to do it. They have generational wealth. So, you've done more in exposing it for me being a native son and knowing what the facts are. And it's helped to me to get rid of some of the emotion. A lot of the emotion. But never anger the emotion is still there. I can't divorce myself from it. And at the same time. I've done my homework. For instance, in terms of what's going on now. I said to you earlier I'm saying again. I've never heard of a federal grand jury probe awards or the possible people they want to talk to the feds want to talk to them, never heard of them being made public the names. What was the conversation on the Housing Commission about the need for access to housing in Hartford? Beyond public housing beyond Bellevue Square and Stowe Village and Ice Heights and Charter Oaks? Here is my thought, the conversation was about it depended on more about the upper class. Property taxes? Like, what do you mean? What I'm saying is the more people they need to do the heavy work, there's more public housing. I'm saying is that even as we speak now, like downtown, those high rises, people living in those houses. Oh, yeah, I know them. I know lots of those people. And what happens is, though I'm taking the emotion out. Now, believe it or not, is that many of those high rises down there were built with public money. Yep. But how many of us know about it? How many of us can afford to live there? But even with those public money, there's some subsidies that are there, last I remember. Initially when I first became a commissioner, there's some subsidies that are there that would allow you to move in there. Let me give you another thing where I'm coming from. It's not just emotional. So I'm a news reporter at WKB radio and WQQW radio. And we exposed the things that the average brother and sister don't see. The average brother and sister, no matter their middle class or whatever don't see. So one day I'm at the top floor of the Hartford Insurance Group. It has floor to ceiling windows over Garden Street. I'm in there. And mind you, I'm a street reporter. Yeah. Were you there for a story? I was just there because, oh, Luke Brown from channel three news invited me. I get in there and I say, "Holy shit." Here at Hartford Insurance Group on the corner of Garden and whatever. I felt like I was a thousand miles away from Hartford. The chaos that I was experiencing personally. I remember. a similar incident was that my wife and I went on a boat ride. Down at Riverfront Recapture. My mother-in-law, my late mother-in-law, was there. So, we're going north on the Connecticut River, To the left is Hartford. She says, "wow, we're in Hartford." I said, "yeah." I don't know if you understand what I'm saying. She'd never seen Hartford from that vantage point? Well first after I left Channel 3, I became a Park Ranger down at Riverfront Recapture. Some moneys had been put in from rich folks to recapture the Connecticut River. Recapture it from what? From pollution. Recaptured from pollution like was polluted. It was folks lived down there. The homeless. And they recaptured it. I became a-- Ranger? A park ranger. I said "wow." What'd you see? I saw sheen. You saw what? I saw sheen that many folks from Bellevue Square even didn't knowjust how beautiful this land is. What's going on down here. I mean, the natural beauty. And what was being done. All of this you gotta demystify the bureaucratic mode of things and check out the politics of it all. You'll find out. Then I did a deep dive, and I'm talking too much but I'm thinking about the definition for politics in our Funk & Wagnalls. It's about power. About power. But then for me to explain in my head why things haven't changed is that human nature. Aren't we all God's children? Well, what am I saying: we're human beings. All that's glitter ain't gold and all that's black ain't so. What does it mean for me? It means hold on to your tradition, no matter where you come from. But don't forget. Don't forget. There's different things I saw in Trenton, New Jersey then Hartford, Connecticut. Not just because I'm a native son here, but we didn't consider Trenton as our home until we went to that family reunion. Well, I think that was a divine happening. When we had Milner and Perry what was the vibe of Hartford like? Vibe was one were one by one? What was that? The vives were one by one. We knew our strength politically. What happened to that? Very simple. Divide and conquer. Why is it so easy to divide and conquer after that particular moment? Because we're all striving to we want the best out of life that we can get. That takes money. That takes money. But see, the ignorance of white supremacy is one whereby even today. How are things able to get like it is in this country today? You get people to believe that there isn't enough when there is, and that if one has, it's because they're going to take it from you. Now let's be real. Basic is all that. That's why people today and because of Hartford, divide and conquer. And a situation whee we're human beings. You're a beautiful human being. You're a beautiful sister. And some people ask me, saying, "well, how do you marry a Polish woman?" No. "How do you marry a white woman?" I said, my wife is not white. I said, "she's Polish." What does that mean? She's traditional Polish. She attended the last Polish college in this country. Alliance. She participated in her tradition. And I'm being very blunt with you. When I lived in Bellevue Square, if you lived outside of Bellevue Square, you wanted nothing to do with us. My self-esteem was always intact because my mother and my father and within our household there. You wouldn't do anything to bring any embarrassment to the household. What I'm saying is that my hormones were all over the place. You're what? My hormones. Like every other teenage kid, right? But what happened was my self-esteem was intact because of my family. And it meant my self-esteem was always intact. I knew I was no Billy Dee Williams or Nat King Cole. Right? And at that time, if you were not high and bright or you played basketball, I looked like a little African refugee reflecting on it. But my pride was there. I had the same inklings. And so, ironically enough, with all I went through after high school, my name became very prominent as a newsman. I had 1 or 2 occasions now, of a sister saying to me, "how do you marry?" I said, "well, first of all, if you know me don't go down that road. I got pride in who I am." "But how do you do it?" I said, and I think it was professor, judging people by the content of their character, not by the color of their skin. But then I have to get real crass. How did I get real crass to my response? Remember when I lived in Bellevue Square? You wouldn't give me no booty nohow. No disrespect. And so not no anger. My father, my mother and my grandmother, I got my grandmother's picture on the wall. My mother always said, "you never treat a woman bad." My standards that I got my house. So, in fact, I judge by the content of the character. I'll never apologize for that. And so that conversation, I had that conversation with a sister, who now Lew Brown, the high profile. But I never participated in that bullshit because I knew it was a dead end when you get caught up in it. Oh, you're different than the rest of them. But when the shit goes down negatively, if you haven't got a strong resolve, strong family you start to participate in the bullshit. Then when the shit goes down, even to the point, even now, Doug McCrory I'm not excusing anything, but Doug was trying to bring the bacon home for his constituents. But I go to the factories that all my years of covering. I've never seen a federal grand jury probe warrant. Get out. I covered with the Italian boys with law enforcement, with the Mafia, organized crime. Never. And lawyers would tell him take the Fifth Amendment. The foundation of this nation is in jeopardy. We are at a crossroads. And what's going to wake us up? I'm hoping that we will form coalitions, put aside those for a minute, those human factors and realize--. One more thing, Hartford despite the large population. Then you mean? Then-- Like when it was at its peak? That's my historical perspective. And I keep on saying to you, you make me thinking the historical perspective. Hartford has had more Black folks than Waterbury. I mean going to the politics. Hear what I'm saying. Hartford has never had a black congressman or congresswoman. You say, "Lew so what?" Waterbury has had two. One a Republican, Gary Franks and Jahana Hayes, a Democrat, two congresspeople. What is it? Well, what it is is that anybody who knows their business in Connecticut is that the cities put their fingers on the scale, if you will. That means you can determine who's going to be the next congressman, the next governor or even the next president. What do you think of the current primarying of Larson. Everybody's got to be held accountable. Now, let me tell you, I know John Larson. John Larson comes from a family of nine in his family, Irish from East Hartford. They lived in public housing. All I'm simply saying, not just John Larson. In a historical perspective, remember when I came to WKB radio through the back door. No college, nothing. Even though I got a scholarship in my name and my wife. Hear what I'm saying to you: education is where it's at. I come through at a time when I was a little freaking nerd. Glasses this thick. Right. And I was shunned amongst males back then. The sound of being intelligent. Your innate abilities. With what? Innate intellect? Your God given. Where were we going? We were talking about Larson and the primary. Oh, Larson. Good man. Hasn't been primaried since he's been in office. Yeah. Are you telling me we haven't had any black candidates? We haven't had any candidates. It hasn't been primaried at all. No one has been successful enough to meet the threshold. Why? When we had the numbers to challenge the Irish boys. Why? All you gotta do is listen to what the hell you're doing. Historically. Why? So, towns, cities control the outcome in districts. In the fifth congressional district, the city is Waterbury. You ever been to Waterbury? Mhm. The bat scene of Waterbury, where they're divided between being Cape Verdeans. That's my bird clock. Oh, okay. Cape Verdeans and Blacks from the South. And understand, don't get me wrong, I understand if you're Cape Verdean in that community in Waterbury. I'm not knocking them. The Cape Verdeans stick together. But when it comes to what you say, when the daughters, you said it, when the daughters, start menstruating. I don't want them around me. I want them around. That's natural. You got to marry a Cape Verdean from Cape Verde. Politics is of such. And if you do a deep dive, the division amongst Black Americans and Cape Verdeans is wide. In my generation here in Hartford didn't have that same problem. The Mendezs in Bellevue Square, The Arangos were Cape Verdean. In fact, the traditional meals, whether it was Cape Verdean, Southern, Irish, Italian, Spanish, Polish. I don't know how to explain it, except that that's my thinking. That's where it goes. Let's talk a little bit about what you wanted to be. So you made it back to school. And what did the horizon look like for you in terms of your ambitions, your goals? What you wanted to be. Happy go lucky, easy go free, nothing in the world bothers me. I was a little nerd. Loved the library. Still do. Do a lot of reading. I think my desire was to not forget from where I came from. And not wanting to rub shoulders. You know blinded by dollar signs. Was that what you felt like your peers were doing? Yeah. You say, how can you say that? I knew that in the street every game was cop and blow. What does that mean? You can make some money. And I still repeat it all the time, all money ain't good money. Do you sell your soul for a dollar? Do you sell your traditions? Your family traditions for dollar? Now many of my peers did when they got that fast money. For control of the block. In the city. So money. I'm not being unrealistic, but you gotta have a strong moral compass, man. You did a lot of part time jobs, right? When you were coming up, you said shoeshine boy. Yeah. Did the shoeshine boys ever have their clients, or how did that go? Did they have their? Like did specific people go to you. Did you have your own clients? Oh yeah. I learned how to be an entrepreneur. On 983 Main Street I have my shoeshine box out there. Okay. On the curb there was a BB, I mean bootblack. At that time, you had to have a license to be a bootblack. Okay. You wrote it on the curb? No, no. That was done by the city. That's by law. BB you couldn't-- Where was this? Where the Stilts Building is now. On the curve itself, on the flat side, on the street side. It was BB. Okay. The guy in charge of it his place was down in the Front Street area. Okay. And Mr. Barnard. Henry Barnard was his name. I mean old from the Thomas Hooker families that came to and settled in Hartford. But oh yeah, I had a client his name his name was Judge Suwalski. He always wore white socks. I never got polish on his socks, but, oh yeah, entrepreneur. So, wait, how does this bootblack work? You go downtown, you pay for your license and then? Pay for your license. Then you go out in the street. And hustle on your own? Well, but see-- I'm asking. I don't know how it works. No, no, no. Let me say it. So you see, the word you used I hustled. But today I say ain't nothing wrong with hustling. You hustle. You hustled and then you develop your own clientele because you didn't get-- You know what I would do? Polish on the judges socks so he comes back to you, right? And when I use the term, it's not just, professor, I would make money and come home, say to mom [Holds out hands]. How much was it to? What were the services? How much? You get a tip. But I think it was about a quarter at that time. Okay. But when I was going to high school, I stopped because I started working in a grinder shop for a mentor of mine. An Italian mentor of mine by the name of William Yacone. And what kind of mentor? An Italian mentor, you said? He's Italian. I say that because, quite frankly, it was organized crime. Organized crime. The numbers, the lottery. These were some of my mentors, as well as the Doc Hurleys of the world, my mentors. And you worked in the grinder shop, you said. Making sandwiches? Making grinders on the corner of Main andMather. And then on Barber Street. Did it move to Barber Street? From Main and Mather up to Barber Street. It moved, you're right. It closed down there when they start developing, redeveloping, I should say. Okay. So bootblack, then you're working in the sandwich shop. You're in high school at this point. What do you want to be? What's your horizon looking like? I was satisfied. Okay. I just knew what I didn't want to do. The foundry? I didn't want to do the foundry. I didn't want to go to jail. I wanted to be around, of course, making money. I was a hustler, not a hustler, I could play pool. Okay? Was there a way to make money from that? Oh, yes. Gambling. Okay. And then most pool halls-- Weren't you too young? Let me put it this away, I was old, I would say. My thinking was not like-- They let you in, though? In the pool hall. You were too young, but they still let you in. In the pool hall. Yeah. Alright. Were you good? The best. Okay. Did you have a special name? I was this high. [Gestures] Did you have a special name? Name of the pool hall was Bob's Pool Hall. Bob's Pool Hall. Bob's Pool Hall. Under the subway restaurant. Both are owned by a Black man. You mean the subway was owned by a Black guy? Yeah. And the pool hall was owned by a Black guy. Yeah. And it was that kind of a situation. Well and my nickname then was Luigi. Oh, okay. Alright, so you made money. You made some money from that. I also worked for the Harford Recreation department. I played a hell of a game of paddle tennis, table tennis. I enjoyed like checker games. Or Chinese checker. Checker games. I didn't play chess, C-H-E-S-S, because it gave me a headache. But I was able to survive in that situation in the streets. I never got shot. You couldn't intimidate me. Okay. And then I'm making grinders. And I end up-- Well, you said you were kind of, like, skinny and nerdy. Yeah. So where did the intimidation come in? Why did people take you seriously? One incident comes to mind, the best way I can tell you this. I can never stand being intimidated or bullied. Back then, guns were not, it was knives. It was knives. Knives, maybe knives, or maybe just bullying. I had one incident where I was bullied there in Bellevue Square. Went to the individual took a bottle and hit him in the head with it. Stood there. Did not run. Another boy, right? Another boy. I never disrespected my sisters. Didn't run. That was a couple of days later after he had bullied me. Okay. Guess what? I didn't run. He looked at me and said, "N-word are you crazy?" "No, no, I got my revenge. You can do what the fuck you want to do with me now, but you will never bully me." Or here's another incident, there was a family in Bellevue Square who lived on the side where the local store was, where you would bring your money or bring bottles in to redeem the bottles. And i used to hustle bottles. One day I went to there to deliver and with the wagon had my bottles. I went to the store, Andy's Market over on Wooster Street, and a young fellow, the young brother from who he was the bully. He was the bully? He was the bully. "I'm taking your bottles." It was in the summertime now. I said., "You're going to do what?" I'm looking right now, I can see him. I started breaking all the bottles I had. Don't ask me where they came from, I don't know. I start breaking the bottles. He looked at me and said, I can still see his face saying, "they say you're crazy. You're crazy." I said, "no, I ain't crazy." I said, "before I let you allow you after I hustled all the the bottles." I said, "you might be able to kick my ass. But I'm going to break every one of these goddamn bottles." He left me alone. "They say you're crazy." Those are my my survival things. And reflecting now. That's the only way I can describe it. So by the time you get to junior/senior year, had you been in trouble with the law? [Shoots a look] I'm going tell you now Carl Hardrick Collegiate Institute at the YMCA. Yeah, but I don't known him. Headed by Leonard Epps. If you want to verify what I'm saying to you go ask Carl Hardrick. Carl, what was the first riot in Hartford? He'll tell you the first riot, outbreak, was in Bellevue Square. I got arrested. Me and a man by the name of George Holter Senior were arrested for I beat the charge, but I spent the night in a lockup. In jail. The next day-- Just the two of you? Me and him. Were arrested? Just the two of us were arrested. I had nothing to do with what was going on there. I'm on this [points to the side]. Cop snapped me. And him neither, right? He didn't have anything to do with it? No, he was in charge. He was the main man. Ralph Holter George? George. But what I'm saying is that I get arrested, spent the night in jail, go to court the next day. Of course, it was a different court setup, but they let me out. I got out and walked home to Bellevue Square. My mom had canaries in our house, in a cage. All I remember is walking through that cage and opening the door to let that canary out of the cage. My mother said to me, I don't know what age I was, I was maybe a sophomore in high school. My mother said to me "come here baby." She knew something, I had been traumatized. Minding your business and then the cops just come and snatch you up? Yeah, but I was able to make relate my personal thing to things I encountered later on in life. I never forgot where I came from. I knew about cop and blow. I knew about wannabe gang member. I knew about my own standards. I could not bring any embarrassment home in my house. For instance, I was little upset when our senior year at Hartford High, they had the social gathering and they had Senior night or something, one of those proms or whatnot. I could never find a girl to go to the prom with. I was upset, but not angry. And there's a difference. Upset because I never go to that prom. Oh, you didn't go to the prom? I didn't go to the prom. Okay. And. But I swallowed it because my Mom, "baby." I said "I'm alright. I'm alright, Mom." But I was the first in my family to graduate from high school. High school? Hartford Public High School. Class of '60. My brother Bob, who was is up in Cape Cod, now up there in the Shriver family. He's very close with the Shriver family. In fact, Tim Shriver was his, so it was a different generation. My brother Bob, you know. But the point being, he was the first to graduate from college in our family. And we had moved. He also went to Hartford High? He went to Hartford High. We had moved to Martin Street, M-A-R-T-I-N, at Martin Street. And what happened was that we would put pennies together coupled with some outside help. So he attended Central Connecticut State University. Okay. How many years older is he? Well, I'm 83 and Bob is 79. But just don't say tell him that. This guy's got some with his age. You can't tell him he's 79? He's very private. Oh. Right. Okay. I think he thinks that the age of people is that-- So you were the first and he was the second. First graduate from high school. And he's the second. He's the second. And what was the trajectory? Where were you going after high school? What was the plan for you? What gave you that idea? I'm good at what I did. Good at hustling all kinds of things. But that's not a career. But you thought you could. I knew I could. You knew you could. So how long did you hustle? And when did you come to the realize? I stopped hustling when I got the job. Which one? In the Post Office. As a letter carrier. How old were you? So you graduate when you're 18, and then you become a letter carrier when? No. What happened was I graduated at 18, and I went to join, after wandering a little bit, I went to join, this is in '60, the Air Force. And I passed the physical and the whole nine yards. Flew out of here from New Haven to San Antonio, Texas, the US Air Force down on a physical training course portion. A Jeep pulls up. Two guys said "they want to see you in the hospital" at Lackland Air Force Base in San Antonio, Texas. I'm there and I go to the patient. Doctor says to me, "how did you get in here?" What for your previous issues? Yes. Your cardiac? Yes. That's what I'm thinking. Oh. How'd you get in here? I said what you mean. You mean how to get here just took the test like everybody else. I said "I took the exam." It was just prior to the Vietnam War. Think about the time factor. And what happened was the FBI was doing investigations, if you were going, you were drafted or you joined or it must have been I don't know exactly, but if you joined the services, if you joined, the FBI would do the, or somebody in the federal government, would do the background check. They said, "how'd you get here?" Like everybody else the physical. "Well, you're not staying here." They gave me an honorable discharge with a medical reason. A medical reason, but an honorable discharge. I got my DD=214 form. It shows it right. Years later, after that incident with Les Payne. Was that the reason? I don't really know. Was that the reason. I don't know. Hey, but even to a point where another incident happened to me when I was at Channel 30 pr was that POP? Ford was the President. So, he was coming to town. So you had to get press credentials back then. I went to the Republican office downtown Hartford, Bushnell Park, to get my press credential. Now, mind you, my career as a reporter I was solid. I'm in there-- You said, you're solid? Solid as a reporter. In my credentials. Right. I know it. But I'm sitting there and two white men, and looked like they stepped out of corn fields of Iowa somewhere, said to me, "we want to talk to you." This going to sound crazy. My instincts told me this ain't, yeah. The receptionist for the Republican Party is sitting right there at that desk. They start asking me questions. The two men? The two men. They said to me, "have you ever been six feet tall?" Listen to me, my sister. I looked at him. I didn't smile one bit. I said to them, "no, not even in my elevator shoes." The receptionist, who was white was back there who worked for the Republican Party. She said "what the." So what I said, I said to her, I said, "don't get these fools excited." I don't know where that came from. I'm related to you, I said. She said, "but." I said, "no, not even my elevator shoes." I looked him in the eyes. In other words, are you through with your bullshit now? I'm walking out of here, but I can't. That that was written in the Connecticut magazine. I don't know if you ever heard of a columnist. A what? A columnist. Columnist by the name of Gail Collins. Gail Collins? Gail, G-A-I-L, she's a columnist or former there at. What did she write? She wrote about that incident? She was writing for, she was a freelancer. Yeah. And at that time, we would work out of the state capitol. I had a room up there. I had a desk up there. She's a freelancer, Gail Collins, and she wrote about in Connecticut Magazine. She wrote about the incident? About the incident. From whose perspective? From my perspective. Did she talk to the secretary, too? I don't know if she did because she didn't talk to me. Right. Gail Collins didn't talk to me because-- So she reported on the incident and didn't-- What happened was the Republicans were running the show then, and they were tight with the media. And somehow this young lady told Gail Collins what had happened to me. It was published in Connecticut Magazine. I've never been able to get my hands on that magazine. Wow. Again, because at that time, I'm doing doing my thing I'm not concerned about bullshit like that, quite frankly. Yeah. Have you ever been sick? When he said now what I want to do, now we're we're reflecting. Yeah. Explain some things to me. Explain some things to me of what happened like in Texas. I want to be in the Air Force. I did, I kept my shoes close. After high school, they say you're out of here. Why? I still got my DD-214 form upstairs. I'm discharged with a medical clause. That was the term. So you leave the Air Force? Yeah. And then what? Like career wise? That's when I did my wandering trying to find myself from Boston, Massachusetts, Back Bay on Mass Avenue to-- Trying to find the job as a dishwasher? Rejected, because at that time was the Boston Strangler. That's the time factor. Then to Trenton, New Jersey, I thought that was going to be my home. Because my grandmother, man. Because I loved her so much. And how long did you stay in Trenton again? About a year. Year or more. But during that time I got a job right away because my cousin got a job for me at Educational Testing Service. Was he a janitor there? He was janitor there. We were all janitors. At that time, ETS was a think tank. What does that mean? That means-- Had they created the SATs already? Yeah. Oh yeah, the SATs and the medical board and all that. Their connections with Princeton University. And so but what happens is it was weird because they were thinkers there. It was a think tank. I learned about think tanks, where the wackiest of people like were employed there. They were when I say wacky, I shouldn't say, but they were highly intelligent people. But they were weird, strange people. And I mean, they were in their own world. How long did you stay there? Over a year or more. And that's, that's when Rider college. Remember Rider? Yeah. Rider. Rider College, I remember Rider College. When you asked me, I think April had come down right when we were talking, My Mom got married to a guy who had always liked her. My Mom worked for a wholesale retail business. In Trenton? No, in Jamaica. Oh, Jamaica? In Jamaica, yeah. So all of us passed the exam to go to high school. And so I think right when she came down, I was saying that my Mom had three tuitions and that you have to pay for, you have to pay for books, and you have to pay for uniforms out of pocket. Everything you have to pay for out of pocket, you have to pay for the bus out of pocket. And it's so funny how small Hartford is, kind of the way we're doing with Trenton, the bus, the owner of the buses, I met him at the West Indian Social Club. Just like felt like the world was collapsing into one. And so, my Mom said that she faced the difficult decision of having three girls. So, once I passed the exam when I was ten and it's time for me to go to from sixth grade onward, you have to pay. She knew she was in trouble because she's got three tuitions, three uniforms, three sizes. That same dynamic is just today. Yeah, in terms of working class folks and providing the best education possible. Where education is always a premium in this country. Yeah. This nation. Now, now we got a guy who's President of the United States can't put two sentences together. Yeah. So she decided that the only way out of her situation was to come to America, where when you're in Jamaica, as you said, it seemed like America is paved with gold. Exactly it's not. It's for all immigrants. That was my conclusion. Listen I've gotta. Yeah. It's already 6:31. Let's see. When do you eat? I need to finish up. Almost done. Yes. I'm downstairs. I'm trying not to disturb you though. Oh, yeah. It's been like, four hours. Yeah, yeah, yeah. So, yeah, I think. Can we finish in about five minutes and then I'll turn the recorder off. Okay. Yeah. Okay. Yeah. So she decided to get married and moved to New Jersey. Where? In Trenton? Yeah, straight to Trenton. Yeah. Okay, so let's finish up part one. Just tell me just sort of from let's conclude the Trenton part and then we'll hand over and then we can do part two another day. Yeah. Come back here? Yeah. I mean-- Yeah, just finish up the the-- Yeah. Can you ask me a question again? Oh, right. So you said you stayed at ETS for a year and then. Realize that I couldn't stand Trenton. Came back to Hartford and got more involved in the grinder shop making pizzas and grinders and so on so forth. I was never allowed to write numbers, and the deciding factor then was that when I had one of my mentors said, no, you're going to get a legitimate job." This happened. And I said, I want to write numbers." He said, "no, you're not writing numbers. You're going to go get a job." Something came about with the post office and I took the test and became a letter carrier. How old were you then? In my late 20s. Okay. But then what happened was I took the test. Didn't expect to pass it. Passed it. Now. I got a job cleaning the post office, and I'm working in the grinder and pizza shop. Two jobs? Yeah. Then I said to him, my mentors, "why do you never let me write numbers?" "Because we knew you had more in you. You're too bright to write numbers." The old Italians now. And that money was--I said, "what do you mean?" He said, "Lew, we knew that was a matter of time, only a matter of time before we would be out of business." This is the illegal numbers. I said, "what do you mean?" He said, "Lew, we figured out there as soon as them crooked politicians get themselves involved and make money allegedly for the people we're out of business." I say to you, professor, what is the lottery all about? The legal lottery. Do we know where the money is going? One last thing. When the law passed, it took us to be involved to passed that law, voting for it. Supporting politicians when they legalized lottery. For two years, that money was supposed to go into property taxes to alleviate the cost of taxpayers for education, for public education. That lasted for two years, maybe two years, three years. After that, the moneys from the lottery went into the municipalities. And to the general funds of the municipalities. And the rest is history. Yeah. Well, let's stop there and we can do part two and we can pick up with that. Can you push the stop button? You see.

lines.line: # Father's Work and Industry Changes

> The speaker discusses their father's employment history, starting with his work at a foundry in Rocky Hill, Connecticut, and later at Philbrook, Booth and Spencer on Homestead Avenue. The conversation covers the closure of these foundries due to environmental laws and changes in industry demands. The speaker also mentions how parts of the buildings are now used by the Salvation Army, reflecting on the broader impacts of industrial decline on the community.

The speaker also mentions how parts of the buildings are now used by the Salvation Army, reflecting on the broader impacts of industrial decline on the community.

lines.line: # Family History and Racial Violence

> The speaker delves into a painful part of their family history involving racial violence in South Carolina, as documented in a book. They recount a story about their grandfather's murder and the subsequent threats to their family, highlighting the long-lasting trauma and silence that followed within the family. This narrative illustrates the deep-seated racial tensions and the personal impact of such events on families.

lines.line: # Reflections on Racism and Discrimination

> The speaker reflects on personal experiences with racism and discrimination, recounting incidents where they faced or observed racial prejudice. They discuss the broader implications of these experiences on their views and interactions with society. The speaker also touches on the emotional and psychological impact of racism, emphasizing the importance of resilience and self-awareness in confronting such challenges.

lines.line: # Urban Development and Housing in Hartford

> The speaker discusses the evolution of housing in Hartford, particularly focusing on public housing developments like Bellevue Square. They explore the political and economic factors influencing urban development, including the role of developers and the impact of federal funding. The conversation also touches on the challenges and changes in housing policies over the years, reflecting on how these developments have shaped the community.

lines.line: # Career Path and Life Choices

> The speaker recounts their various part-time jobs during their youth, including working as a shoeshine boy and in a sandwich shop. They discuss their decision to join the Air Force, which was cut short due to medical reasons, and their subsequent employment at the post office. This narrative highlights the speaker's journey through different jobs and the influences that shaped their career path, reflecting on the importance of finding meaningful work and making ethical choices.

lines.line: # Father's Work at Connecticut Foundries and Environmental Changes

> The speaker recounts his father's employment at the Rocky Hill and Homestead Avenue foundries in Connecticut. He describes how these foundries eventually closed due to changing times and stricter EPA regulations. The conversation touches on the decline of local industry and the resulting impact on the community, including the current vacancy of the buildings and the loss of jobs.

lines.line: # Foundry Work and Family Artifacts

> The speaker explains the nature of the work at the foundry, including the production of iron items such as sewer tops. He shares a personal story about receiving a heavy iron bookend made by his father, which was returned to him years later by someone who recognized his family connection. This artifact serves as a tangible link to his father's labor and the legacy of foundry workers.

lines.line: # Family History and Lynching in South Carolina

> The speaker introduces the book 'Carnival of Blood' and reads a passage detailing a violent incident involving his family in Denmark, South Carolina. He reflects on the trauma of his grandfather's lynching, the threats made against his family, and the silence that followed due to fear and pain. The speaker discusses how this history was validated by newspaper accounts and how it shaped his family's legacy.

lines.line: # Family Resilience, Migration, and Generational Experiences

> Despite the trauma in their family history, the speaker describes his father's resilience and refusal to be bitter. He talks about his brother becoming a police officer, his father's complex feelings about authority, and the strong family bonds that helped them persevere. The narrative includes anecdotes about his father's work ethic and the family's migration from the South to the North.

lines.line: # Childhood, Family Values, and Memories in Bellevue Square

> The speaker shares memories of his childhood, including his father's early work hours and the lessons he learned about responsibility and respect. He recalls visits to his grandmother in Trenton, her strong character, and the family's close-knit dynamics. Stories about family interactions, such as his grandmother's handling of money and her wisdom, highlight the values that shaped his upbringing.

lines.line: # Interracial Marriage, Self-Esteem, and Community Attitudes

> The speaker addresses questions about his interracial marriage to a Polish woman, emphasizing her cultural background and his own strong sense of self-worth. He reflects on community attitudes toward interracial relationships and the importance of judging people by character rather than race. The speaker credits his family for instilling values that helped him navigate social pressures and maintain his identity.

lines.line: # Foundry Closures, Environmental Hazards, and Hartford Development

> The discussion returns to the closure of the foundries due to environmental hazards like asbestos and lead. The speaker explains the economic challenges of redeveloping contaminated land and the reluctance of developers to invest in such properties. He also reflects on the changing financial landscape for Hartford developers, who once brought significant private capital but now rely more on public funds.

lines.line: # Public Housing in Hartford and Cabrini-Green Analogy

> The speaker discusses the perception of public housing in Hartford, noting that it was seen as permanent rather than temporary. He draws parallels to the failures of high-rise public housing projects like Cabrini-Green in Chicago, referencing studies on overcrowding and social problems. Drawing on his experience as a housing commissioner, he reflects on policy changes and the persistent challenges faced by residents.

lines.line: # Hartford Politics, Power, and Racial Dynamics

> Reflecting on his time as a housing commissioner, the speaker describes the persistent inequities in Hartford's political and economic systems. He discusses the influence of generational wealth, the role of black governors, and the manipulation of public trust. The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding power dynamics and the need for coalition-building to address systemic issues.

lines.line: # Housing Access, Downtown Development, and Class Divides

> The conversation shifts to the broader issue of housing access in Hartford, including the development of downtown high-rises with public money. The speaker notes that many residents are unaware of the subsidies involved and that few can afford to live in these new developments. He shares his perspective as a reporter, describing the stark contrasts between different parts of the city and the persistent class divides.

lines.line: # Riverfront Recapture and Hartford's Natural Beauty

> The speaker recounts his experiences seeing Hartford from new vantage points, such as the top of the Hartford Insurance Group and from the Connecticut River. He describes working as a park ranger during the Riverfront Recapture project, which aimed to restore the river from pollution and make it accessible to the public. He reflects on how many residents, especially from Bellevue Square, were unaware of the city's natural beauty and the efforts to reclaim it.

lines.line: # Hartford's Black Political Power and Divisions

> The speaker reminisces about a time when Hartford's black community had significant political strength and unity. He discusses how divide-and-conquer tactics eroded this power, leading to fragmentation and competition for resources. The conversation explores the challenges of maintaining solidarity and the impact of systemic racism on community cohesion.

metadata.title: Hartford's Black Political Power and Divisions

lines.line: # Hartford's Lack of Black Congressional Representation and Political Structure

> The speaker compares Hartford to Waterbury, noting that despite Hartford's large black population, it has never had a black congressperson, while Waterbury has had two. He explains how cities influence political outcomes in Connecticut and discusses the divisions within black communities, including distinctions between Cape Verdeans and African Americans. The speaker reflects on the relative unity in Hartford compared to other cities.

lines.line: # John Larson, Political Accountability, and the Speaker's Education

> The conversation turns to Congressman John Larson, his long tenure without a primary challenge, and the lack of black candidates meeting the threshold to run. The speaker shares his personal journey into journalism without formal education, highlighting the value of innate intelligence and the importance of education for social mobility. He reflects on the political landscape and the barriers to change.

lines.line: # Pool Halls, Recreation, and Standing Up to Bullies

> The speaker recounts his time working in pool halls, playing games like paddle tennis and checkers, and making money through gambling. He shares stories about dealing with bullies in Bellevue Square, emphasizing his refusal to be intimidated and his willingness to stand up for himself. These experiences contributed to his resilience and street smarts.

metadata.title: Pool Halls, Recreation, and Standing Up to Bullies

He shares stories about dealing with bullies in Bellevue Square, emphasizing his refusal to be intimidated and his willingness to stand up for himself. These experiences contributed to his resilience and street smarts.

lines.line: # Trouble with the Law and High School Experiences

> The speaker discusses being arrested during a riot in Bellevue Square, spending a night in jail, and the emotional impact on him and his mother. He reflects on his commitment to not bringing embarrassment to his family, his disappointment at not attending prom, and his pride in being the first in his family to graduate from high school. The narrative also touches on his brother's educational achievements.

lines.line: # Street Life, Hustling, and Early Jobs

> The speaker describes his early ambitions, the realities of street life, and the importance of maintaining a moral compass. He talks about his experiences as a shoeshine boy, learning entrepreneurship, and the value of hard work. The narrative includes details about the licensing system for bootblacks and the relationships he built with clients.

metadata.title: Street Life, Hustling, and Early Jobs

lines.line: # Post-High School Wandering, Military Experience, and Discrimination

> After high school, the speaker describes his attempt to join the Air Force, being discharged for medical reasons, and his subsequent wandering in search of work. He recounts an incident where Republican officials questioned his credentials in a discriminatory manner, and how this was later reported by journalist Gail Collins. These experiences highlight the obstacles he faced due to both health and systemic bias.

lines.line: # Family Migration, Education in Jamaica, and Parallels to Hartford

> The interviewer shares her mother's story of migrating from Jamaica to the United States to provide better educational opportunities for her children. She describes the financial challenges of paying for tuition, uniforms, and transportation in Jamaica, and draws parallels to the struggles of working-class families in Hartford. The conversation highlights the universal desire for upward mobility through education.

lines.line: # Return to Hartford, Avoiding Illegal Activity, and Entering the Post Office

> Upon returning to Hartford, the speaker explains how his mentors discouraged him from getting involved in illegal numbers running, believing he had greater potential. He describes taking the civil service test, becoming a letter carrier, and balancing this job with work in a grinder and pizza shop. The speaker reflects on the transition from informal hustling to stable, legitimate employment and the broader implications of legalized gambling.

children.text: # Father's Work at Connecticut Foundries and Environmental Changes

> The speaker recounts his father's employment at the Rocky Hill and Homestead Avenue foundries in Connecticut. He describes how these foundries eventually closed due to changing times and stricter EPA regulations. The conversation touches on the decline of local industry and the resulting impact on the community, including the current vacancy of the buildings and the loss of jobs.
# Foundry Work and Family Artifacts

> The speaker explains the nature of the work at the foundry, including the production of iron items such as sewer tops. He shares a personal story about receiving a heavy iron bookend made by his father, which was returned to him years later by someone who recognized his family connection. This artifact serves as a tangible link to his father's labor and the legacy of foundry workers.
# Family History and Lynching in South Carolina

> The speaker introduces the book 'Carnival of Blood' and reads a passage detailing a violent incident involving his family in Denmark, South Carolina. He reflects on the trauma of his grandfather's lynching, the threats made against his family, and the silence that followed due to fear and pain. The speaker discusses how this history was validated by newspaper accounts and how it shaped his family's legacy.
# Family Resilience, Migration, and Generational Experiences

> Despite the trauma in their family history, the speaker describes his father's resilience and refusal to be bitter. He talks about his brother becoming a police officer, his father's complex feelings about authority, and the strong family bonds that helped them persevere. The narrative includes anecdotes about his father's work ethic and the family's migration from the South to the North.
# Childhood, Family Values, and Memories in Bellevue Square

> The speaker shares memories of his childhood, including his father's early work hours and the lessons he learned about responsibility and respect. He recalls visits to his grandmother in Trenton, her strong character, and the family's close-knit dynamics. Stories about family interactions, such as his grandmother's handling of money and her wisdom, highlight the values that shaped his upbringing.
# Interracial Marriage, Self-Esteem, and Community Attitudes

> The speaker addresses questions about his interracial marriage to a Polish woman, emphasizing her cultural background and his own strong sense of self-worth. He reflects on community attitudes toward interracial relationships and the importance of judging people by character rather than race. The speaker credits his family for instilling values that helped him navigate social pressures and maintain his identity.
# Foundry Closures, Environmental Hazards, and Hartford Development

> The discussion returns to the closure of the foundries due to environmental hazards like asbestos and lead. The speaker explains the economic challenges of redeveloping contaminated land and the reluctance of developers to invest in such properties. He also reflects on the changing financial landscape for Hartford developers, who once brought significant private capital but now rely more on public funds.
# Public Housing in Hartford and Cabrini-Green Analogy

> The speaker discusses the perception of public housing in Hartford, noting that it was seen as permanent rather than temporary. He draws parallels to the failures of high-rise public housing projects like Cabrini-Green in Chicago, referencing studies on overcrowding and social problems. Drawing on his experience as a housing commissioner, he reflects on policy changes and the persistent challenges faced by residents.
# Hartford Politics, Power, and Racial Dynamics

> Reflecting on his time as a housing commissioner, the speaker describes the persistent inequities in Hartford's political and economic systems. He discusses the influence of generational wealth, the role of black governors, and the manipulation of public trust. The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding power dynamics and the need for coalition-building to address systemic issues.
# Housing Access, Downtown Development, and Class Divides

> The conversation shifts to the broader issue of housing access in Hartford, including the development of downtown high-rises with public money. The speaker notes that many residents are unaware of the subsidies involved and that few can afford to live in these new developments. He shares his perspective as a reporter, describing the stark contrasts between different parts of the city and the persistent class divides.
# Riverfront Recapture and Hartford's Natural Beauty

> The speaker recounts his experiences seeing Hartford from new vantage points, such as the top of the Hartford Insurance Group and from the Connecticut River. He describes working as a park ranger during the Riverfront Recapture project, which aimed to restore the river from pollution and make it accessible to the public. He reflects on how many residents, especially from Bellevue Square, were unaware of the city's natural beauty and the efforts to reclaim it.
# Hartford's Black Political Power and Divisions

> The speaker reminisces about a time when Hartford's black community had significant political strength and unity. He discusses how divide-and-conquer tactics eroded this power, leading to fragmentation and competition for resources. The conversation explores the challenges of maintaining solidarity and the impact of systemic racism on community cohesion.
# Hartford's Lack of Black Congressional Representation and Political Structure

> The speaker compares Hartford to Waterbury, noting that despite Hartford's large black population, it has never had a black congressperson, while Waterbury has had two. He explains how cities influence political outcomes in Connecticut and discusses the divisions within black communities, including distinctions between Cape Verdeans and African Americans. The speaker reflects on the relative unity in Hartford compared to other cities.
# John Larson, Political Accountability, and the Speaker's Education

> The conversation turns to Congressman John Larson, his long tenure without a primary challenge, and the lack of black candidates meeting the threshold to run. The speaker shares his personal journey into journalism without formal education, highlighting the value of innate intelligence and the importance of education for social mobility. He reflects on the political landscape and the barriers to change.
# Pool Halls, Recreation, and Standing Up to Bullies

> The speaker recounts his time working in pool halls, playing games like paddle tennis and checkers, and making money through gambling. He shares stories about dealing with bullies in Bellevue Square, emphasizing his refusal to be intimidated and his willingness to stand up for himself. These experiences contributed to his resilience and street smarts.

> The speaker discusses being arrested during a riot in Bellevue Square, spending a night in jail, and the emotional impact on him and his mother. He reflects on his commitment to not bringing embarrassment to his family, his disappointment at not attending prom, and his pride in being the first in his family to graduate from high school. The narrative also touches on his brother's educational achievements.

story.description: This transcript appears to capture a deeply personal and historical interview, touching on a wide range of topics, including family history, racial dynamics, and societal changes. Central themes include the impact of industrial decline and environmental regulations on communities, as illustrated by the foundries in Connecticut. Personal anecdotes feature prominently, such as the interviewee's father's work life, experiences with racism, and the family's historical trauma from racial violence. The interview also delves into broader reflections on politics, community, and personal resilience amidst socio-economic challenges. Additionally, it touches upon dilemmas around identity and belonging, particularly concerning race and heritage.
story.title: Lew Brown part II Interview 08/11/2025 - 6:36:50 pm
